You are an expert public policy analyst reviewing sustainability research articles. **Instructions:** - Answer ALL questions only based on the provided Codebook and Article - Provide specific citations when requested - Keep justifications concise and evidence-based - For Yes/No questions, choose definitively based on evidence - For Yes-or-No or multiple choice problems, answer from the given options only (the options are in parentheses) - Format your entire response using the XML template below to ensure each answer stays inside its tag. It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. Billiet Center for Environmental and Energy Law, Ghent University, Gent, Belgium Thomas Blondiau Center for Economic Studies, KU Leuven, Leuven, Belgium Sandra Rousseau Faculty of Business and Economics, KU Leuven, Campus Brussel, Brussel, Belgium Abstract We analyze judicial policy lines concerning the punishment of environmental crime using a unique European dataset of individual criminal cases, including case-speciﬁc information on offenses and offen- ders. We investigate policy choices made by lower criminal courts, as well as their follow-up by the relevant court of appeal. The sanctioning policy of the courts has proven to be varied as well as consistent. Judges carefully balance effective and suspended penalties, most often using them cumulatively, but in speciﬁc cases opting to use them as substitutes. Overall, both judges in lower and appeal courts balance environ- mental law and classic criminal law and aim at protecting individuals and their possessions as well as the environment. Keywords: criminal sanctions, environmental crime, judicial policy. 1. Introduction In a world where ﬁrms and individuals do not automatically comply with legislation, monitoring and enforcement strategies are necessary elements of an effective and well-designed environ- mental policy. As a recent overview by Gray and Shimshack (2011) of the empirical evidence on the effectiveness of environmental enforcement in the US shows, monitoring and enforcement activities generate not only reductions in violations, but also signiﬁcant reductions in emissions. In general, enforceable regulations are often cited as a dominant factor explaining the substantial improvements in environmental quality in developed countries over the last four decades (see e.g. Kagan et al. 2003). Enforcement typically involves a range of policy choices, such as monitoring strategies by state or federal agencies (e.g. Rousseau 2007), self-policing by facilities (e.g. Toffel & Short 2011), and the imposition of administrative and criminal sanctions, as well as civil sanctions (e.g. Gray & Shimshack 2011). One of the less documented phases of the environmental enforcement process is the sanctioning policy applied by criminal courts. In this contribution, we construct a comprehensive picture of judicial policy lines in the punishment of environmental crime in the Correspondence: Sandra Rousseau, Faculty of Business and Economics, KU Leuven, Campus Brussel, Warmoesberg 26, Brussel B-1000, Belgium. Our dataset, a collection of individual criminal cases that is unique in Europe, includes case-speciﬁc information on offenses, offenders, and imposed sanctions, and follows cases up to the appeal phase. We investigate three decisions made by criminal judges in the courts of ﬁrst instance: First, whether a prosecuted defendant should be acquitted; second, whether the offense should be punished with an effective penalty, a suspended penalty, or a combination of both; and ﬁnally, what the level of the effective and/or suspended penalty should be. We then analyze whether appeal judges conﬁrm or adapt the initial verdicts and, if verdicts are adapted, we study the size and direction of these changes. Judicial behavior has been extensively studied from a behavioral law and economics view- point (e.g. Guthrie et al. 2001). Prior to the emergence of this strand of literature, Marks (1988) was one of the ﬁrst to state that behavior of judges can be examined in a rationality-based setting. Since the 1980s, a signiﬁcant body of theoretical research has been developed to understand judicial behavior (see Spiller & Gely 2007 for a US based overview). Yet most studies are framed within a common law context. Thus, not all results, for instance, those relating to the importance of judicial precedence (Rasmusen 1994; Levy 2003), carry over to a civil law context. Fon and Parisi (2006), for example, state that judicial precedent can be a central component of judicial decisionmaking in civil law countries, but that its inﬂuence depends on the demand for consis- tency with previous case law. The rule of precedence can even become irrelevant, when the law gives judges broad discretionary freedom. Typically this applies to the determination of sanc- tions within the criminal law system of civil law countries: judges have extensive discretionary freedom when deciding on the type and level of sanctions in criminal cases, and, thus, the rule of precedence is not utilized in this subject area. With regard to Belgium, a civil law country, this analysis is corroborated by a recent study by Monsieurs et al. (2009) who surveyed Belgian judges with criminal caseloads and found that the inﬂuence of judicial precedent is very limited in the sanctioning decision process. An overview of the determinants of monetary sanctions imposed in practice following environmental offenses is provided by Rousseau (2009). Based on international empirical evi- dence, she studies the determinants and levels of administrative, criminal, and civil ﬁnes inﬂicted for environmental offenses. Three major categories of variables are identiﬁed: the circumstances of the offense, offenders’ characteristics, and indirect institutional effects. Some general trends emerge: ﬁnes increase with the harm caused by the offense (Earnhart 1997; Kleit et al. 1998; Oljaca et al. 1998) and ﬁnes are higher for repeat offenders, as well as for intentional offenses (Kleit et al. 1998; Oljaca et al. 1998). The studies discussed also indicate that institutional factors matter (Earnhart 1997; Ringquist 1998; White 2006). However, only a couple of empirical studies deal with criminal sanctions for environmental offenses, as opposed to administrative and civil sanctions. In one of the ﬁrst empirical studies dealing with criminal environmental sanctions, Cohen (1992) analyzed the ﬁnes imposed by US federal criminal courts on ﬁrms sentenced between 1984 and 1990. He found that the particular type of violated legislation had a signiﬁcant impact on the size of the ﬁne imposed on convicted corporations. Large corporations received higher ﬁnes than small ones. Further, Billiet and Rousseau (2003) performed an analysis of the jurisprudence of the Court of Appeal of Gent (Belgium) for the period 1990–2000 concerning wastewater discharge permits. The ﬁnes imposed in the ﬁrst instance were higher when the defendant had a criminal record and for infractions on the Flemish Environmental Permitting Act 1985. In Flanders, as in the rest of the EU, environmental permitting legislation is a centerpiece of environmental regulation. Billiet and Rousseau (2003) also found that, contrary to the rulings in the ﬁrst instance, appeal judges considered both the violator’s intentions and the harm caused to third parties. Finally, Blondiau Punishing environmental crimes C. M. Billiet et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 473 Page 3: and Rousseau (2010) studied the impact of a judge’s objective function on criminal sanctions (ﬁne or plant closure) inﬂicted to corporate entities to enforce environmental standards. Using a subset1 of the dataset used here, they found that judges explicitly take social sanctioning costs into account next to environmental damages. While the previously discussed studies yield interesting insights into sanctioning decisions for environmental offenses, none of them provide a detailed picture of judicial policy lines in determining criminal sanctions. They do not explicitly take the variety of different judicial policy options available to judges into account, nor do they study the complete path of criminal cases up to the appeal court. Thus, our current study analyzes judicial sanctioning decisions in a more general framework by including the different sanctioning possibilities, such as the option to postpone a conviction and the use of suspended or effective sanctions, from ﬁrst instance level to appeal. The analysis allows us not only to identify broad trends in criminal judicial decisionmaking, such as leniency toward offenders that took positive actions to limit damages, but also to investigate the speciﬁc factors determining sanctioning decisions for particular offenses, such as violations of environmental permitting requirements. More- over, we are able to study the use of suspended sanctions by criminal judges: are such sen- tences used as substitutes for effective sentences or are both types of sentence used cumulatively? For instance, we show that the cumulative use of effective and suspended sanc- tions is the general policy line when judges deal with corporate entities. Finally, we identify the changes made by appeal judges to ﬁrst instance rulings and are, therefore, able to comment on the consistency in sanctioning decisions through the different layers of the criminal court system in Belgium. In section 2 we describe the legal background to the criminal sanctioning of environmental offenses in Flanders. In section 3 we formulate the hypotheses that will be empirically tested. The data, model, and variables used in the analysis are summarized in section 4, while the results are presented in section 5 and discussed in section 6. Section 7 concludes. 2. Background of the criminal sanctioning of environmental offenses in Flanders As our empirical analysis deals with the criminal enforcement of environmental legislation in Flanders, Belgium, we provide a short overview of the most relevant characteristics of the criminal sanctioning system in that country.2 2.1. Prosecution decision Public prosecutors in Flanders decide to prosecute in about eight percent of the environmental cases that reach their ofﬁce.3 Prosecution guidelines regarding the decision to prosecute and the type and level of sanctions to request from the bench do not exist in Belgian criminal procedure law. In May 2000, the Council of Prosecutors-General approved a memorandum identifying the environmental offenses that rate priority in the prosecution policy (Environmental Prosecution Memorandum). The prioritized offenses are essentially those that have, or might have, serious consequences for public health and the environment, have an organized crime character, are committed in a professional context, or concern the exploitation of a facility or activity without the required environmental permits. This memorandum, however, does not bind the prosecu- tors at all. Thus, the prosecutor’s discretion concerning the decision to prosecute and, if pros- ecuting, the selection of sanctions to request, is very broad. Punishing environmental crimes C. M. Billiet et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 474 Page 4: 2.2. Criminal sanctioning decision Criminal court judges have three main options to shape a sanctioning policy. Firstly, if the criminal court ﬁnds facts and liability proven, which occurs in nine out of 10 cases, the court has to choose between a postponement of conviction and an actual conviction. Postponement of conviction, basically a choice not to punish, is subject to a probation period of one to ﬁve years in which the offender must not reoffend. Secondly, if the criminal court opts for a conviction, it has to impose at least one principal sanction. Belgian criminal law has three principal sanctions, which all have a punitive character: imprisonment, ﬁnes, and community service.4 It is legally possible, and common, to impose more than one principal penalty, for instance, combining a ﬁne with a prison sentence (Billiet & Rousseau 2011). For each selected principal penalty, the judge also needs to determine the penalty level within the minimum and maximum limits set by the legislator. The range of legally allowed penalties is typically very broad. The statutes used in our dataset provide for ﬁnes ranging from a minimum of 26 euro to a maximum of 10,000,000 euro and for prison sentences with a minimum of eight days and a maximum of ﬁve years (Billiet et al. 2009). The Belgian Criminal Code includes a conversion mechanism of prison sentences to ﬁnes that applies whenever the offender is a legal entity. We used this mechanism in our analysis to convert prison sentences imposed on convicted individuals to monetary equivalents.5 The last main policy choice of the criminal court relates to the possibility to suspend penalty execution, partially or completely. As a postponement of the verdict, a suspension of penalty execution is always linked with a probationary period of a maximum of ﬁve years. Both options put a clear emphasis on individual deterrence. Suspension of the execution of penalties is a prevalent option in criminal courts throughout the European Union, where it is commonly seen as a means to prevent recidivism (Commission of the European Communities 2004). A criminal judge can also impose one or more additional – punitive or remedial – sanctions,6 if and when he has imposed at least one principal penalty, suspended or otherwise. Most often, judges disregard this option and shape their environmental sanctioning policy through principal penalties only, ﬁne-tuning them by using the aforementioned three main options. Thus, criminal judges develop a policy within very broad legal margins. Moreover, they are not guided by sentencing guidelines as such guidelines do not exist in Belgian criminal law. Criminal judges are also not bound by the public prosecutor’s sanctioning request nor, as mentioned earlier, by the penalty imposed in previous similar cases. The only decisive factor in the sanctioning decision is the criterion of proportionality with “the seriousness of the offense:” the criminal judge has to punish “in proportion to the seriousness of the offense.” This basic sentencing criterion,7 which was developed by Belgian Supreme Court and which applies to all types of criminal cases, includes two sub-criteria: the objective gravity of facts as such and the culpability of the defendant. The gravity of the facts is rated by the extent to which the unlawful activities harmed or might have harmed the public interest protected by the violated legislation. Looking at Belgian legislation, the public interest that the environmental statutes aim to protect from harm always consists of (an aspect of) the natural environment (e.g. water, air, biodiver- sity), which is protected for anthropocentric reasons (with a strong focus on health issues), or because of the intrinsic value of the natural environment, or for both of these reasons. The assessment of and the importance attached to harm by the Belgian Supreme Court are in line with the focus on harm in the existing law and economics literature on optimal penalties (e.g. Polinsky & Shavell 1979, 1992; Garoupa 2001; Rousseau & Telle 2010). The theory of marginal deterrence, developed by Shavell (1992) and Mookherjee and Png (1994), which holds that optimal penalties rise with the harmfulness of acts and should reach the extreme only for the Punishing environmental crimes C. M. Billiet et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 475 Page 5: most harmful acts, constitutes an approach that matches the proportionality principle set out by the Supreme Court. To conclude, the actual and potential harm associated with the prosecuted offense, as well as the culpability of the offender, are two key factors in determining the appli- cable penalty. 2.3. Some additional key features Since the 19th century, the Belgian Supreme Court has adamantly upheld the requirement of an intentional element (guilt) in order to conclude that an offense can be punished by criminal law.8 Strict liability does not belong to the sanctioning logic. Further, since 1999, legal persons are criminally liable and can be prosecuted and punished together with natural persons involved in the same criminal case. Under the Belgian Criminal Code, legal persons incur criminal liability directly as entities on their own. Thus, it is not necessary to identify one or more natural persons, be it leading persons or employees, who have been committing offenses for the legal person (Van den Wyngaert 2009; Vermeulen et al. 2012). Individuals, as well as corporate entities, who consider themselves harmed by the offense under consideration can become a civil party in the criminal case. If the defendant is convicted, the judge will also rule on civil claims and, if necessary, award damages. Judgments pronounced by the court of ﬁrst instance can be appealed by each of the con- cerned parties with the competent court of appeal. If the prosecutor appeals, which he syste- matically does when a defendant appeals, the appeal judge is completely free to determine punishment: he can reduce, conﬁrm, or increase the verdict pronounced by the judge of ﬁrst instance. The Criminal Prosecution Code obliges criminal judges to explicitly motivate the choice, as well as the severity, of each of the sanctions they impose, whenever they have discretion to choose between two or more sanctions, and to set the level of a selected sanction between a legal minimum and maximum. The motivation is allowed to be short, but must be precise. This obligation typically applies for all principal penalties. In contrast to some other countries, the Belgian legal system does not have specialized environmental prosecutors, nor specialized environmental courts.9 Yet as in the rest of Europe and the world (Pring & Pring 2009), there is an increasing demand for such specialization as a result of the complexity of environmental issues and legislation. 3. Hypotheses In this section, we formulate three hypotheses concerning judicial policy in the punishment of environmental crime. These are then confronted with practice using our dataset for Flanders. As previously discussed, the criminal judge has to punish in proportion to the gravity of the facts, which primarily aims at the actual and potential harm those facts caused (see e.g. US Sentencing Commission 1993, 2008; UK Sentencing Advisory Panel 2000). Actual and potential harm can be measured in several ways. However, not all measures are equally objective and easy to assess. Some measures for harm are easy to grasp, such as whether efforts were made to put an end to the offense or to clean up the pollution caused, and the presence of civil parties in the case seeking compensation for harm suffered, while others require a more specialized knowledge, such as whether the offense violated noise regulations. Tangible and measurable harm, such as a given amount of waste, can more readily be assessed than other more hidden types of harm, such as soil and groundwater contamination. Given that Belgian criminal courts are not specialized in environmental crime, but deal with a variety of cases, judges might rely more heavily on objective case elements that require little specialized knowledge. Thus, our ﬁrst hypothesis is: Punishing environmental crimes C. M. Billiet et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 476 Page 6: Hypothesis 1: When judges have little speciﬁc environmental expertise, easily veriﬁable factors will have more weight on the level of the imposed penalty than less tangible factors. Besides the level of the penalty, judges can also choose between effective and suspended penalties. Suspended penalties were introduced in Belgium to solve the practical problems related to short prison sentences (Van den Wyngaert 2009). Using suspended sentences, instead of effective sanctions, could mitigate problems, such as insufﬁcient room in prisons and the difﬁculty of integrating ex-convicts into society, without completely compromising the deterrent effect of the sanction. The use of suspended penalties soon spread from prison sentences to criminal ﬁnes. Consistent with the historical roots of suspended penalties, the general idea, supported by the country’s high courts (e.g. Constitutional Court 2004/105 and 2008/157) and literature (e.g. Van den Wyngaert 2009), is that the use of suspended penalties expresses leniency: judges would substitute effective sanctions by suspended sanctions, which implies a negative link between the level of the effective sanction and that of the suspended sanction. However, this interpretation of the policy tool is not mandatory. Thus, logically, judges might also use effective and suspended sanctions cumulatively as two speciﬁc parts of an imposed sanction. This leads to a second hypothesis: Hypothesis 2: When suspended penalties are considered as a less stringent substitute for effective penalties, the stringency of effective and suspended penalties has to be negatively correlated. While in principle institutional factors should not matter, the evidence suggests that in practice they do. For instance, a Republican president in power in the US led to lower environ- mental ﬁnes (Ringquist 1998). The sanctioning procedures matter as well, for example, ﬁnes increased with the number of defendants in White (2006). Rousseau and Billiet (2005) showed that the judging decisions in the Court of Appeal of Gent were based on different characteristics than the judging behavior in the lower courts. More than lower courts, higher courts tend to preserve the core principles and goals of the law submitted to them. Throughout all different types of criminality, the hard core of the judicial work of criminal courts is common criminal law. In Belgium, common criminal law centers around 19th century ideas of personal guilt as the reason for punishment and the necessity to limit the right of the state to punish its citizens to the most essential values of society. Besides the security of the nation, the goals our common criminal law recognizes as essential are the protection of individual property and of the indi- vidual’s life and physical integrity. Rousseau and Billiet (2005) found that this typical criminal law rationale surfaced in the judicial policy of the Court of Appeal of Gent regarding offenses concerning wastewater permits, even if the environmental statutes involved aimed at different goals: the protection of the environment for anthropocentric reasons (public health), as well as the intrinsic value of the natural environment. Thus, our third hypothesis is: Hypothesis 3: When higher courts focus on the preservation of the core principles of law, judgments from the courts of ﬁrst instance regarding environmental offenses are more likely to reﬂect the core principles of environmental law, while judgments from the court of appeal regarding such offenses are more likely to reﬂect the core principles of criminal law. These hypotheses are confronted with reality by testing the inﬂuence of variables relating to the gravity of the facts, the harm they caused, the protection of the property and well-being of individuals, as well as the culpability of the offender on the type and level of criminal sanctions imposed by the judges. Next we discuss the model that is estimated (4.2) and deﬁne the dependent (4.3) and the explanatory variables (4.4). 4.1. Description of the dataset To document the criminal decision process in Flanders, we studied all verdicts of environmental case law at seven courts of ﬁrst instance and the Court of Appeal of Gent, from 2003 to 2007 (Billiet et al. 2009, 2011).10 The different environmental statutes enforced in the dataset are listed in Appendix I. We collected data on 1,034 cases, of which 912 were decided by the courts of ﬁrst instance, while 122 were concluded on appeal. In total, 1,617 defendants were tried in these 1,034 criminal prosecutions: 1,352 only in the ﬁrst instance, and 265 both in the ﬁrst instance and on appeal. Some 80 percent of the defendants were individuals, while 20 percent were legal entities. Because each defendant can face several accusations, the cases include 3,561 accusations, of which 3,004 were dealt with in the ﬁrst instance and 557 on appeal. A limited number of legislative texts dominate the case law: over two in three accusations involve violations of the Flemish Environmental Permitting Act 1985 and the Flemish Waste Act 1981.11 The other charges that were brought to court mainly concern violations of manure and noise legislation. Judgments also contain information on the type of pollution or nuisance that took place. Waste problems (34 percent) and noise nuisance (14 percent) are most frequently cited, followed by water pollution (nine percent) and soil contamination (seven percent). Descriptions of the harm that was caused are scarce. When harm is explicitly mentioned, the decisions refer in general to damage done to public health or the health of third parties (eight percent combined). Environmental damage to fauna and ﬂora is stated less often (ﬁve percent in total), while damage to the property of third parties is hardly mentioned at all (less than one percent). In the ﬁrst instance, judges convict three in four defendants, one in eight is acquitted, and for the remaining defendants the conviction is postponed. The appeal judges convict a similar number of defendants (three in four), but appear to acquit more defendants (one in six). Looking at the type of sanctions, we ﬁnd that a monetary ﬁne is by far the most used criminal sanctioning instrument, as it is imposed in over 95 percent of convictions. For corporations, the average ﬁnes – including the legal correction factor – amount to 14,569€ in the ﬁrst instance and 10,733€ on appeal. For individuals, the average ﬁnes are signiﬁcantly lower: 3,787€ in the ﬁrst instance and 8,061€ on appeal. Also, for individuals, it is noteworthy that in 10 to 15 percent of the convictions, a prison sentence (combined with a ﬁne) is imposed (see Table 1). The average duration of a prison sentence is 4.4 months in the ﬁrst instance and 6.2 months on appeal. 4.2. Description of the estimation We investigate the main policy choices made by judges once the facts are established and the imputation issue is solved. We analyze sanctioning decisions concerning corporate entities separately from those concerning individual defendants. To correct for a possible sample selec- tion bias, we ﬁrst estimate a probit model to study the decision to acquit or convict a defendant. Next, we estimate two ordinary least squares models to analyze the level of the effective sanction and the level of the suspended sanction, respectively, each including the inverse Mill’s ratio based on the preceding probit results (see Fig. 1). To deal with the issue of identiﬁcation, we use the ratio of proven accusations to the total number of accusations (PROVEN ACC) as an independent variable to explain the decision to acquit or convict a defendant. This variable is not Punishing environmental crimes C. M. Billiet et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 478 Page 8: included to explain the sanction levels, as judges cannot base their sanctioning decisions on non-proven accusations. Postponements of verdicts are included in the analysis as zero effective and zero suspended sanctions. The effective sanction is included as a random regressor in our speciﬁcation of the suspended sanction. It was not possible to use an instrumental variable approach or a seemingly unrelated regressions approach in our analysis because both effective and suspended sanctions are – and legally should be – explained by identical regressors. Legally, we cannot identify any reason why effective and suspended sanctions should be determined by even one different factor and, thus, our model reﬂects this reality. After the analysis of the sentences formulated in the ﬁrst instance, we estimate the probability that an appeal is initiated either by one or more of the defendants, by civil parties, or by the public prosecutor. Again, the probit estimation of the probability that a verdict was appealed is necessary to correct for a possible sample selection bias through the inclusion of the inverse Mill’s ratio in the later estimations. To explain the probability of appeal we include the level and the square of the effective sanction, while these variables are not included in the estimation of the changes in sanction levels.We study how the appeal judges modify the verdicts imposed by lower courts and we explicitly distinguish between the changes in effective sanctions and the changes in suspended sanctions. Thus, we estimate six functions in total for each group of defendants, that is, corporate entities and individuals (see grey coloring in Fig. 1). 4.3. To calculate the level of the sanctions imposed by the criminal court, we look at the sum that the offender actually needs to pay, including the legal correction factor. Thus, the depen- dent variables for these analyses are: “APPEAL” indicating whether at least one of the parties appealed the initial verdict (APPEAL = 1); “DIF-EFFSAN” expressing the change (in euro) between the effective sanction on appeal and the effective sanction initially imposed in the ﬁrst instance; and“DIF-SUSSAN”expressing the change (in euro) between the suspended sanction on appeal and the suspended sanction imposed in the ﬁrst instance. Deﬁnition of the explanatory variables To investigate the judicial sanctioning policy in more detail, we analyze the impact of several explanatory variables12 on the decision processes. To a large extent, these explanatory variables aim at measuring the impact of the seriousness of the offense on the sanctioning policy, including the gravity of the facts (i.e. actual and potential harm), as well as the culpability of the offender. Further, we include some variables concerning the type of offender and to control for differences between courts and for time trends. A summary of the deﬁnitions of dependent and explanatory variables can be found in Appendix II. 4.4.1. Variables concerning the type of offender The offender is a corporate entity, an individual offending during his/her professional activities (PROF = 1), or an individual offending during his/her private activities (PROF = 0). Our dataset includes 17 percent corporate entities, 35 percent “professional,” and 48 percent “private” offenders. 4.4.2. Variables concerning the seriousness of the offense We describe the seriousness of the offense using three categories: (i) variables mainly dealing with the gravity of the facts; (ii) variables that concern both the gravity of the facts and the culpability of the offender; and (iii) variables mainly concerning the culpability of the offender. Variables concerning the gravity of the facts. The total number of accusations a defendant has to answer for is given by the variable “COUNT ACC.” The variable “PROVEN ACC” represents the total number of proven accusations incorporated in the verdict. In our analysis, only descriptive information concerning proven offenses is used to construct the variables of the sanctioning policy, as only those offenses lead to punishment. In the ﬁrst instance (appeal), each offender was held liable on average for 2.1 (2.7) proven accusations. The variable “DURATION” expresses the length of the longest-lasting offense in days. The average duration of the longest lasting offense was 505 days in the ﬁrst instance. The agency typically focuses its monitoring activities on facilities that are relatively more damaging to public health and the environment. Further, we know whether one or more of the offenses committed by the offender classiﬁes as a prioritized offense in line with the aforementioned Environmental Prosecution Memoran- dum (PRIORITY = 1). Some 23 percent of the offenders committed at least one prioritized offense, based on ﬁrst instance verdicts. The judgments can also describe the damage caused by the offenses. Thus, we identify offenses damaging vulnerable ecosystems, fauna, or ﬂora (NATURE = 1), and offenses damaging public and/or private health (HEALTH = 1). In the ﬁrst instance, 3.6 percent of the offenses were harmful to nature and eight percent were harmful to health. On appeal, these percentages increase to 4.6 percent and 27 percent, respectively. The indicator variable “CIVIL PARTY” represents whether one or more civil parties, seeking compensation for damage caused to themselves or their property, were involved in the case or not. In the ﬁrst instance, one or more civil parties were involved in the case of approximately 15 percent of the offenders; on appeal the percentage was 26 percent. As a rule, civil parties are private individuals; however, occasionally they include local or regional public authorities. We know whether the offender violated the obligation to have a valid environmental permit (PERMIT = 1) or the conditions stated in the environmental permit (PERMIT-COND = 1). As mentioned previously, permitting is a centerpiece of environmental legislation in Flanders and, generally speaking, in the European Union. Looking at the type of pollution associated with the offense, we distinguish six different types by deﬁning the appropriate indicators: offenses related to illegal waste treatment (WASTE); groundwater or soil pollution (SOIL-GROUND); noise pollution (NOISE); odor hindrance (ODOR); air pollution (mostly relating to dust) (AIR- DUST); or surface water pollution (WATER). Variables concerning both the gravity of the facts and the culpability of the offender. The indicator variable “POSITIVE” reﬂects whether the judgment mentioned if the offender took measures to remediate, clean up, or solve the damages caused by the offenses. In the ﬁrst instance, some form of positive action was acknowledged for 23 percent of the offenders. Variables concerning the culpability of the offender. The judgment can mention whether the offender was previously convicted (RECORD = 1) for environmental or non-environmental offenses. In the ﬁrst instance, 13 percent of offenders had a criminal record. To measure the attitude of the offender, we use the indicator “INTENT.” The variable equals one if the judgment included at least one of the following terms: “knowingly and willingly,” “unwillingness,” “pur- posefully,” “determined,” or “purpose.” In the ﬁrst instance, 11 percent of offenders scored positive on this variable. The judgment often explicitly mentioned if an offender acted in pursuit of gain and ﬁnancially beneﬁted from the offense. The indicator “GAIN-SEEK” equals one if the written motivation of the verdict used at least one of the terms: “economic beneﬁt,”“economic gain,” “ﬁnancial gain,” “pursuit of gain,” “pursuit of proﬁt,” or “proﬁtable.” In the ﬁrst instance, 17 percent of offenders scored positive. 4.4.3. Control variables Finally, we include a number of control variables to investigate the presence of systematic differences in judicial policies in different courts and in different years. The indicator variables “BRUGGE,” “GENT,” “KORTRIJK,” “OUDENAARDE,” and “WESTHOEK,” respectively, reﬂect whether the case was ruled on by the ﬁrst instance court of Brugge, Gent, Kortrijk, Oudenaarde, or in the Westhoek (i.e. Ieper and Veurne). The reference category is the court of ﬁrst instance in Punishing environmental crimes C. M. Billiet et al. © 2013 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 481 Page 11: Dendermonde. The variables “YEAR04,” “YEAR05,” and “YEAR06” represent whether a verdict was pronounced in 2004, 2005, or 2006, respectively (2003 is used as reference). 5. First we analyze judgments made by the courts of ﬁrst instance for corporate entities and individuals, respectively, (5.1) and then we check if and how these judgments are subsequently changed by the appeal court for both groups of defendants (5.2). 5.1. Courts of ﬁrst instance To capture several dimensions of criminal enforcement, we study when prosecuted corporate entities and individuals are more likely to be acquitted and, if they were not acquitted, we estimated the level of the effective sanction, as well as the level of a suspended sanction. 5.1.1. Probability of acquittal Unsurprisingly, we ﬁnd a highly signiﬁcant negative impact of the fraction of proven accusations compared to the total number of accusations on the probability of being acquitted for corporate entities (see Table 2), as well as individuals (see Table 3). Moreover, corporate entities were less likely to be acquitted when prosecuted for an offense relating to the duty to have an environmental permit or conditions imposed by such a permit, and when tried in Gent. They were more likely to be acquitted when tried in the Westhoek. Individuals had a lower probability of acquittal when offending in the course of professional activities and when prosecuted for an offense against environmental permit conditions. Their opportunity to be acquitted rose with the number of accusations faced and in the Westhoek. 5.1.2. Effective sanction We now focus on the effective sanction, namely the sum of the effective ﬁne and the monetary equivalent of the effective prison time, for corporate entities (Table 2) and individuals (Table 3). Ceteris paribus, corporate entities received signiﬁcantly higher effective sanctions when they committed prioritized offenses, in cases including civil parties, for waste offenses, offenses causing odor hindrance or water pollution, when they acted with intent, or from a ﬁnancial motive, and when tried in Gent. They received lower effective sanctions when they undertook action to put an end to the offense and its negative effects. Individuals received signiﬁcantly lower effective sanctions when they offended in their professional capacity, took action to put an end to the offense and its negative effects, or were convicted in Brugge or Kortrijk, or in the year 2004. 5.1.3.
